subject: emergency-medicine
title: Envenomation
preambleMarkdown:
# Envenomation



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Envenomation is a medication or pharmacology topic in emergency medicine.
- This article focuses on triage, immediate threats, time-sensitive diagnostics, disposition, and safety-netting.
- The practical clinical priority is therapeutic benefit balanced against toxicity and interactions.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Envenomation to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Envenomation is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is therapeutic benefit balanced against toxicity and interactions.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Drug class and mechanism.
- Therapeutic indication and line of therapy.
- Route, formulation, onset, duration, metabolism, and elimination.
- Expected adverse effects, dangerous toxicities, interactions, and special-population precautions.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Envenomation depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In emergency medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Envenomation.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Envenomation disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Envenomation should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Envenomation first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Envenomation, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Patients may present with the condition being treated, lack of response, adverse effects, overdose, withdrawal, or interaction.
- Important history includes dose, timing, adherence, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status, allergies, alcohol or substance use, and other medications.
- Medication harms may mimic disease progression, so compare symptom timing with drug exposure.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Envenomation, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused emergency medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Envenomation presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Envenomation with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Envenomation is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Envenomation should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm indication, contraindications, dose, route, duration, and monitoring before starting or continuing therapy.
- Adjust or avoid therapy for renal impairment, hepatic impairment, pregnancy, lactation, age, allergy, and interactions.
- Manage toxicity by stopping exposure, supporting physiology, using antidotes or reversal when indicated, and monitoring delayed effects.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Envenomation is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses therapeutic benefit balanced against toxicity and interactions.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Choose therapy by mechanism, target condition, expected benefit, toxicity risk, interactions, and monitoring burden.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use drug levels, ECG monitoring, decontamination, dialysis, reversal, or antidote pathways only when they change management.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Envenomation, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Envenomation may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Envenomation patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Envenomation, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Envenomation in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Envenomation, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Envenomation does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Envenomation may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Envenomation, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Envenomation as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know mechanism, main use, major contraindication, dangerous toxicity, monitoring test, and key interaction.
- Renal and hepatic function often decide dose safety.
- A medication list is part of the diagnostic workup, not an administrative detail.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Envenomation draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Venomous Snakebites

_Source lines: 39656-39851._

```text
V. VENOMOUS SNAKEBITES
A. Discussion. Venomous snakes inhabit all states with the exceptions of Maine,
Alaska, and Hawaii. In addition, exotic venomous snakes are kept in zoos and pri-
vate collections throughout the United States. Therefore, the potential for bites by
venomous snakes exists everywhere.

1. Incidence. Each year in the United States, over 8,000 victims of venomous
snakebites are recorded; 5 or 6 die each year.

2. Identification of snakes. If possible, the type of snake responsible for the
snakebite should be identified to determine whether the snake is venomous
and the type of venom injected.

a. Venomous snakes may be classified as crotalids (pit vipers), elapids, or
colubrids. Most venomous snakes in the United States are pit vipers.

i. Crotalids or pit vipers are classified in three groups in the United
States: the rattlesnake, the water moccasin ("cottonmouth"), and the
copperhead. Characteristics of pit vipers include:
a) Small "pit" indentation between the snake's eye and nostril
b) Vertical slit pupils
c) Arrowhead-shaped head
d) A single caudal row of plates from the anal plate to one-third of the
way from the tail (nonpoisonous snakes have two rows of plates)
e) May have "rattles" on the tail (in rattlesnakes)
f) Movable front fangs
ii. Elapids are snakes with fixed front fangs (e.g., coral snakes). Coral
snakes are small and shy and have red and black bands that are wider
than the interspaced yellow rings; the red band is surrounded by
yellow bands.

iii. Colubrids have hind fangs. None are native to the United States,
although they are found in exotic collections.

b. Harmless snakes have round eyes, oval heads, two rows of plates near the
anal plate, and no pit.

3. Pathogenesis. Many venomous snakes have long, sharp hollow fangs.

a. Crotalid venom is composed of a mixture of enzymes, polypeptides, and
glycoproteins that, when injected, causes tissue destruction, hemolysis,
Quick 	HIT
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy
may be controversial but
should be considered in
symptomatic smoke inhala-
tion and/or if carboxyhemo-
globin #25%.

Quick 	HIT
Any patient with significant
respiratory distress follow-
ing smoke inhalation will
need observation for at least
24 hours.

Quick 	HIT
A person is nine times more
likely to die from lightning
than a snake bite.

Quick 	HIT
"Red on yellow, kills a fellow
(coral snake); red on black,
venom lack (harmless milk
snake)."

484 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Environmental Emergencies
nerve damage, capillary damage, and breakdown of the host cells and
coagulation factors.

b. Coral snake venom, composed of a strong neurotoxin and several enzymes,
causes systemic neurologic symptoms but very little local tissue damage.

B. Clinical features
1. Severity of symptoms is related to:
a. Amount of venom released
b. Type of snake (strength of venom varies)
c. Age and size of snake
d. Age and size of victim
e. Prior health of victim
f. Location of the bite (bites to the head and trunk are three times as danger-
ous as bites to the extremity)
g. Treatment time and type received
2. Local effects
a. Oozing nonclotting blood at site of bite indicates envenomization.

b. Immediate severe pain out of proportion to the wound suggests pit viper
envenomation.

c. Numbness may occur following envenomation by coral snakes.

d. Local swelling occurs within several hours. Edema, cyanosis, hemorrhagic
blebs, and lymphangitis may occur and may spread progressively.

3. Systemic effects
a. Shock and hypotension
b. Compartment syndrome (see Chapter 18.VII)
c. Fluid shift
d. Hemolysis
e. Coagulopathies
f. Petechiae and bleeding
g. Pulmonary edema
h. Neurotoxicity, seizures, altered mental status (especially with coral snake
and Mojave rattlesnake)
C. Differential diagnoses. Most snakebites are a memorable experiences, but some victims
are unable to recall the event. Unless obvious signs and symptoms of venomous snake-
bite are present, consideration should be given to a "dry bite" without venom, other an-
imal or insect bites, local trauma with cellulitis or other infection, and systemic illness.

D. Evaluation
1. Snake identification. Efforts should be made to safely retrieve and identify the
snake.

2. Laboratory studies. For severely symptomatic victims, the tests ordered should
include a CBC with platelet count; blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, and electro-
lyte levels; coagulation profile; urinalysis; and blood type and cross match.

3. Diagnostic tests and imaging studies. An ECG and appropriate radiographs,
including chest radiographs, are indicated.

E. Therapy
1. Prehospital management
a. Transport. The victim should go rapidly to a hospital. Attempts should be
made to calm the patient to prevent the spread of venom. If possible, the
patient should be carried, with the site of the bite immobilized and placed
in a dependent position.

b. The use of ice, incision and suction, tourniquets, electrical shock, and
administration of antivenin should be avoided by prehospital providers.

Hospital management is best, hence the saying, "A set of car keys is the best
first aid for snakebite."

2. ED management
a. Stabilization. Management of the patient's ABCs, with fluid resuscitation for
shock and hypotension, should be initiated.

b. Examination. The patient should be examined carefully to determine the
extent of injury. Mark the skin to identify the rate of spread of erythema
Quick 	HIT
"A snake in hand may save
your tush."

E N V I R O N M E N T A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	485

Environmental Emergencies
and swelling, observing closely for the development of compartment syn-
drome. Monitor for systemic signs such as hypotension, pulmonary edema,
coagulopathy, and neurologic disturbances.

c. Prophylaxis. Tetanus toxoid should be administered as needed. The use of
prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics is recommended by many authori-
ties, but studies supporting the administration of antibiotics are rare.

d. Debridement. The site of injury should be cleansed and debrided.

e. Antivenin is the definitive treatment for snake envenomation. Antivenin
is historically an equine antibody solution that binds and neutralizes the
harmful components of snake venom. CroFab (BTG International Inc., West
Conshohocken, PA) is a newer ovine antibody solution used for crotalid
bites in the United States.

i. Indications. Antivenin administration is indicated for significant enven-
omations. There is a four-grade classification system used to evaluate
severity of envenomation: from grade I (minimal symptoms, no anti-
venin required) to grade IV (very severe, with rapid swelling, ecchymo-
sis, CNS symptoms, convulsions, and shock). Grades II and III typically
have administration of antivenin in order to prevent progression.

ii. Administration. Antivenin should be administered within 4 hours if
possible. After 12 hours, the risk-benefit ratio is questionable. Exam-
ples of usual dose ranges are 2 to 4 vials for grade II envenomation and
15 or more vials for grade IV envenomation.

iii. Adverse reactions can range from mild urticaria to severe anaphylaxis;
however, CroFab has a much lower side effect profile than previously
used horse serum and is even used in minor envenomations.

iv. Contraindications. Antivenin should not be administered if the patient is
definitely sensitive to serum and the pit viper bite is within grade I or II.

However, if it is a severe grade III or IV bite, lack of antivenin therapy
could be fatal.

F. 	Disposition. All patients with serious snakebite envenomations should be hospi-
talized for continued care and observation. If the snakebite is minor, and no sys-
temic effects or significant local findings are found, and no other toxin is present
(i.e., alcohol) the patient can be discharged home with close observation.
```

### Source Excerpt: Insect and Arachnid Bites and Stings

_Source lines: 39852-40220._

```text
VI. INSECT AND ARACHNID BITES AND STINGS
A. Introduction. Problems caused by the bites and stings of insects and arachnids in-
clude primary toxicity from envenomation, local infection, immediate hypersensitiv-
ity to the venom, delayed hypersensitivity, and transmission of infectious diseases.

B. Black widow spider bites

## 1. Discussion

a. The black widow spider (Latrodectus mactans) is found throughout the
United States (except Alaska). It lives in protected locations such as wood-
piles, basements, and garages.

b. Description. Both male and female are venomous, but only the female can en-
venomate a human. The female is twice as large as the male, with a body about
1 cm long, and a total length, including legs, of 3 cm. The body is glossy black
with the classic "hourglass" red shape located on the central abdomen.

c. Pathogenesis. The most toxic component of the venom is a neurotoxin that
causes depletion of acetylcholine from nerve terminals, leading to diffuse
muscle spasm.

d. Risk for severe reaction. Small children and infants are at increased risk for
severe reaction because of their small body size. Adults with preexisting ill-
ness also are at greater risk.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms
i. Local. A sharp pinprick may be felt from the spider's bite, but often, the
bite is not remembered by the victim. A dull and crampy pain, warmth,
or numbness develops around the bite and slowly spreads.

486 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Environmental Emergencies
ii. Systemic symptoms include dizziness, nausea, headache, itching, in-
creased salivation, and weakness. Intense pain from abdominal muscle
cramps may simulate an acute abdomen. Upper extremity bites may
cause chest wall cramps that simulate an acute infarction or other
serious disorder. Symptoms begin to resolve after several hours.

b. Physical examination findings may include severe muscle spasm, ptosis,
edema, hypertension, and fever.

3. Differential diagnoses include bites of other insects or animals and punc-
ture wounds. Systemic symptoms can mimic an acute abdomen or serious
chest pain etiology, dystonic reactions, tetanus, strychnine poisoning, or
hypocalcemia.

4. Evaluation. The history may not reveal the bite; a thorough examination and
wide differential should be maintained.

a. Laboratory studies. All patients with serious symptoms require a CBC,
serum electrolyte panel, blood urea nitrogen and creatinine levels, clotting
studies, and urinalysis.

b. Diagnostic tests include local imaging, chest radiograph, and an ECG.

## 5. Therapy

a. Prehospital management may include placing ice on the bite wound to
reduce swelling and symptoms. If safety permits, the spider should be
brought to the hospital.

b. ED management
i. Stabilization. The patient's vital signs should be monitored, and life
support measures should be instituted as indicated.

ii. Local cleansing of the bite and tetanus prophylaxis are required.

iii. Muscle relaxants and analgesics are used to treat pain and muscle
spasm with controversial use of a slow intravenous calcium infusion.

iv. Antivenin should be administered to very old and very young victims,
pregnant women, patients with preexisting illness, and seriously symp-
tomatic victims. Adverse reactions include horse serum sensitivity,
which can cause serum sickness, anaphylaxis, and even death.

a) Horse serum sensitivity. The patient should be asked about a history
of sensitivity or previous reaction to horse serum.

6. Disposition. All patients with serious signs and symptoms require admission to
the hospital and close observation. If asymptomatic after 2 hours, the patient
may be sent home with instructions to return to the ED if any symptoms
develop.

C. Brown recluse spider bites
1. Discussion. The brown recluse spider (Loxosceles reclusa) is located predomi-
nantly in the South Central United States. It is brown and smaller than the
black widow (about 2 cm in overall length) and usually has a violin-shaped
mark on the back of the cephalothorax.

2. Clinical features
a. Local effects. The initial bite may feel sharp, or it may cause little or no
pain. Pain gradually develops after 1 to 2 hours. An erythematous area sur-
rounded by a white area of vasoconstriction may appear. A central dark-red
blister or bleb with a "bull's-eye" appearance may form. The lesion slowly
grows in size, with rupture of the bleb and formation of an ulcer after sev-
eral days. A black eschar then forms over a large tissue defect (Figure 21-3).

Pain can be severe.

b. Systemic effects. Although local destruction of skin and subcutaneous
tissue is the hallmark of the brown recluse bite, the victim may also develop
chills, fever, malaise, nausea, and vomiting. Children, and rarely adults, may
develop intravascular hemolysis, hemorrhage, disseminated intravascular
coagulation, thrombocytopenia, renal failure, and death.

3. Differential diagnoses include other insect bites, puncture wounds, local
infection, foreign body, and cutaneous manifestation of infectious or systemic
disease.

Quick 	HIT
Close examination of the
skin following a black widow
envenomation may reveal
two small fang marks.

Quick 	HIT
All patients without a history
of horse serum sensitivity or
previous reaction should be
tested for sensitivity with a
dilute intradermal injection
of serum.

E N V I R O N M E N T A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	487

Environmental Emergencies

## 4. Evaluation

a. History and physical examination lead to suspicion or confirmation.

Observation and repeated examination over several days may be needed
to confirm the diagnosis. Early diagnosis is difficult without a positive
identification of the spider.

b. Laboratory tests. CBC, electrolytes, urinalysis, coagulation studies, and
cardiac monitoring are indicated if the patient has signs of systemic
involvement or a confirmed bite with symptoms. Type and cross-match and
transfusion of blood may be required for patients with severe hemolysis.

5. Therapy. Treatment of the brown recluse bite is controversial, but local sup-
portive care and careful cleansing with soap and water are important. Tetanus
prophylaxis should be administered as needed. Vital signs and urinary output
should be closely monitored.

a. Medications. Analgesia and muscle relaxers are keys to proper therapy.

Others with varying degrees of success include steroids, antihistamines,
antivenin, dapsone, and antibiotics.

b. Wound management is a mainstay of treatment, although excision of the
wound has not been shown to improve outcomes.

## 6. Disposition

a. Discharge. If no symptoms are present and suspicion of a brown recluse
bite is low, outpatient management is acceptable.

b. Admission. All patients with signs of envenomation should be admitted to
the hospital and monitored closely for hemolysis or other complications.

D. Scorpion stings
1. Discussion. The scorpion is a nocturnal arachnid found in the Southwestern
United States. It has a stinger in its tail with two venom glands. Most species are
relatively harmless, and their sting usually causes a localized reaction such as
occurs with a bee sting. However, the bark scorpion (Centruroides sculpturatus)
has a neurotoxin in its venom that can cause a severe reaction. This dangerous
scorpion is found on trees in Arizona and New Mexico.

2. Clinical features
a. Local effects. The symptoms from the C. sculpturatus scorpion sting include
immediate severe pain at the sting site, swelling, and, later, numbness. The
injured area is hypersensitive, and the involved extremity may be paralyzed.

b. Systemic effects. The neurotoxin is strongly cholinergic and can cause
excessive salivation, blurred vision, muscular spasms, hypertension, and
respiratory difficulties.

3. Differential diagnoses include snakebite, puncture wound or other trauma,
insect or spider bite, or drug intoxication.

4. Evaluation. Typically, the offending scorpion is seen or assumed by history; if
safety allows the scorpion to be brought in, this is best. Due to wide range of
symptoms and quick progression, thorough history and physical are necessary.

Quick 	HIT
Children are at greatest risk
for complications of scorpion
envenomation, and they may
develop respiratory compro-
mise within 30 minutes.

Quick 	HIT
Scorpions most often sting
the extremities, especially
the feet after hiding in vic-
tim's shoes.

F I G U R E
21-3 	Brown recluse bite with swelling and
necrosis of surrounding tissue.

488 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Environmental Emergencies

## 5. Therapy

a. Prehospital management includes rapid transportation of the patient, applica-
tion of an ice pack to the sting site, and safe transport of the scorpion for iden-
tification. If severe symptoms occur, life support measures should be initiated.

b. ED management
i. Antivenin should be administered in all cases of severe envenomation.

ii. Ventilatory support may be required, with intubation and oxygen for
patients with severe systemic response or anaphylaxis.

iii. Atropine may be required to counteract the cholinergic effects; the dose
is titrated to relieve the cholinergic signs.

iv. Benzodiazepines may be used for seizures and muscle spasms.

6. Disposition. All victims should be observed for 24 hours, especially children.

Symptomatic patients should be transferred to the intensive care unit if symp-
toms are severe.

E. Hymenoptera stings

## 1. Discussion

a. Hymenoptera include honeybees, wasps, hornets, yellow jackets, fire ants,
and harvester ants.

b. Identification of the offending insect can be difficult.

2. Pathogenesis. The venom injected may contain histamine, serotonin, amines,
phospholipase, hyaluronidase, and other substances; the components vary with
the insect type. Toxic reactions to Hymenoptera insect stings are quite common
in the United States, and types are discussed in Clinical Pearl 21-7.

3. Differential diagnoses include infection, local trauma, foreign body, and skin
disorder. Few patients forget a painful sting, but identification of the exact
insect may be difficult. If anaphylaxis is present, the history may be impossible
to obtain; in such cases, the skin should be carefully searched for sting sites.

4. Evaluation. If moderate or severe symptoms are present, a CBC, electrolytes,
ABG, chest radiograph, and ECG should be obtained. Cardiac monitoring and
close observation are indicated.

## 5. Therapy

a. Local reactions. If a stinger is present in the wound, it should be scraped
out (not squeezed). The wound should be thoroughly washed and ice packs
placed. Oral antihistamines and analgesics should be administered to relieve
discomfort. For moderate swelling, elevation and use of oral steroids for
several days are indicated.

Five Types of Reaction to
Hymenoptera Stings
1. Local reaction consists of significant edema, pain, and erythema at the sting site. If the sting site is
around the mouth or throat, airway obstruction may occur. Some insects can sting repeatedly and cause
local tissue damage, blisters, and severe pain.

2. Toxic reaction. If there are 10 or more stings, a systemic reaction may develop because of the large
toxin load. Vomiting, dizziness, syncope, edema, and diarrhea may develop. Multiple stings may result in
convulsions and death. Africanized bees ("killer bees") that are now in the Southern United States actu-
ally have weaker envenomations than other bees, but they may cause more illness and death because the
victim is stung many times.

3. Anaphylactic reaction is the major cause of death associated with bee or wasp stings. The allergen
component of single or multiple stings may cause an antigen-antibody, immunoglobulin E-mediated
systemic anaphylactic reaction. Histamine, slow-reacting substance of anaphylaxis, and other factors are
released that within minutes produce generalized urticaria, pruritus, dry cough, and wheezing. Severe
symptoms include dyspnea, bronchospasm, cyanosis, cramps, nausea and vomiting, laryngeal stridor, hypo-
tension, shock, loss of consciousness, and death.

4. Delayed reaction consists of serum sickness-like symptoms of headache, malaise, generalized pruritus,
fever, and polyarthritis. These symptoms appear 10 to 15 days after a sting.

5. Unusual reaction. Rare reactions include encephalopathy, vasculitis, neuritis, and autonomic dysfunction.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	21-7
E N V I R O N M E N T A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	489

Environmental Emergencies
b. Anaphylactic reactions are treated with local care, intravenous fluids, anti-
histamines, and steroids.

i. If life-threatening symptoms occur, epinephrine should be administered.

ii. Albuterol nebulizer treatments are indicated in bronchospasm. If severe
airway compromise exists, endotracheal intubation may be indicated.

iii. Hypotensive patients require large volumes of intravenous fluids and
may need vasopressors.

## 6. Disposition

a. Discharge. Patients with minor local reactions may be treated symptomati-
cally and discharged.

b. Admission. Any patient with a systemic reaction should be treated, observed
closely, and admitted for continued symptoms. Patients with anaphylactic
reactions require intensive care monitoring in the hospital.

c. Long-term management is indicated for patients with serious reactions to
Hymenoptera stings. The patient should be referred to an immunologist for
desensitization.

Quick 	HIT
Encourage severely allergic
patients to carry epinephrine
autoinjector.

Medicolegal Considerations
491
22
Peter Van Ligten
MEDICOLEGAL
CONSIDERATIONS
I. INTRODUCTION
The American legal system allows those who feel they have been wronged to argue
their cases in court.

A. Criminal versus civil law
1. Criminal law. A crime is a wrong committed against the public or the public
good. For an action to be a crime, there must be both an intent to commit
the illegal act as well as the commission of the act itself. A criminal action
is brought by the government against the wrongdoer to seek a sanction
(e.g., a monetary fine or incarceration). The crime victim is not a party to the
criminal action and usually does not receive any compensation for resulting
losses or injuries. Seldom is criminality an issue in treating patients. As long as
the patient consents to medical care, charges such as assault (i.e., confrontation
causing reasonable apprehension of unpermitted personal contact) or battery
(i.e., unpermitted personal contact) rarely occur.

2. Civil law. Civil cases (torts) involve wrongs committed against private parties.

When a patient or family member is angered by an interaction with a health
care provider, he or she may bring a lawsuit against the physician and hospital.

The remedy sought in a civil action is usually monetary compensation for the
injured party. Although many claims can be made in a lawsuit, the most fre-
quent tort is that of negligence .

B. Medical negligence is the failure to follow acceptable standards of care in a way
that results in injury to the patient. Medical negligence is the most common claim
in a malpractice lawsuit (see Clinical Pearl 22-1).

1. Avoiding malpractice. Excessive diagnostic testing will not reduce the likeli-
hood of being sued (see Clinical Pearl 22-2).
```
